file_name: Arshad et al. - 2021 - A computer-aided diagnosis system using deep learning for multiclass skin lesion classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (fine-tuning ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 models) and machine learning (skewness-controlled SVR for feature selection, various classifiers) for multiclass skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and presents an original experimental study proposing a new automated framework for multiclass skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (data augmentation, fine-tuning ResNet-50/101, feature fusion, skewness-controlled SVR), experiments on the HAM10000 dataset, and reports original results including accuracy metrics (91.7%, 95%) and computational times, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Computer-Aided Diagnosis System Using Deep Learning for Multiclass Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated computer-aided diagnosis framework for multiclass skin lesion classification to address the challenges of manual dermoscopic inspection. The methodology involves data augmentation of the HAM10000 dataset, followed by feature extraction using fine-tuned ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 deep learning models. Extracted features are fused via a modified serial-based approach and refined using skewness-controlled Support Vector Regression (SVR) for feature selection. The system was evaluated on the augmented HAM10000 dataset, achieving a maximum accuracy of 95.0% with feature fusion and 91.7% with feature selection. These results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning and machine learning hybrid approach significantly improves classification performance compared to non-augmented baselines and recent studies.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass skin lesion classes (Akiec, Bcc, Bkl, Df, Mel, Nv, Vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset using deep learning (ResNet-50, ResNet-101) and machine learning classifiers (LSVM, QSVM, CSVM, etc.). The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories for seven types of skin lesions. Accuracy metrics (e.g., 95.0%, 91.7%) and confusion matrices are reported for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of highly imbalanced dermoscopic images of skin lesions, originally containing over 10,000 images. In this study, it was augmented using rotation, left-right flip, and up-down flip operations to balance the classes.

use.training: The original dataset was augmented to create a new balanced dataset with 42,000 images (6,000 per class), which was then used to train fine-tuned ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 models via transfer learning.
use.testing: The augmented dataset was used for evaluation/testing to compute accuracy metrics (91.7% for feature selection, 95.0% for feature fusion).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for validating the proposed framework and evaluating classification performance.
To visualize and compute per-class performance metrics such as recall, precision, and false-negative rates.
Used as a heuristic search-based feature selection method to refine fused feature vectors by selecting features based on skewness thresholds and fitness functions.
Used in the calculation of skewness for feature selection and in the Standard Error of the Mean (SEM) calculation for feature fusion.
Computed to determine a threshold for feature fusion, comparing individual feature values against the mean of the fused feature vector.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on accuracy metrics and computational time without statistical significance testing.

Experiment 1 (Fine-tuned ResNet-50).notes: CSVM achieved the highest accuracy. CKNN had the lowest computational time. Confusion matrix diagonal values for CSVM: Akiec (96%), Bcc (93%), Bkl (87%), Df (97%), Mel (86%), Nv (94%), Vasc (99%).

Experiment 2 (Fine-tuned ResNet-101).notes: CSVM achieved the highest accuracy. WKNN had the lowest computational time. Confusion matrix diagonal values for CSVM: Akiec (96%), Bcc (92%), Bkl (85%), Df (98%), Mel (86%), Nv (93%), Vasc (99%).

Experiment 3 (Feature Fusion via SbE).notes: ESD achieved the best accuracy of 95.0%. Confusion matrix diagonal values for ESD: Akiec (97%), Bcc (94%), Bkl (89%), Df (98%), Mel (89%), Nv (99%), Vasc (99%).

Experiment 4 (Feature Selection via Skew.notes: Feature selection reduced computational time significantly compared to fusion but decreased accuracy. Confusion matrix diagonal values for ESD: Akiec (94%), Bcc (91%), Bkl (85%), Df (93%), Mel (83%), Nv (97%), Vasc (99%).

Baseline (Non-Augmented).notes: Performance of augmented dataset was better compared to the original imbalanced dataset.

Comparison with Recent Studies.notes: The proposed method showed improved accuracy compared to recent studies.

ResNet-50.structure: Five residual blocks with multiple layers in each block; identity mapping bypass shortcuts
ResNet-50.modified_layers: Last fully connected (FC) layer removed; new FC layer added with 7 outputs connected to Softmax

ResNet-101.structure: 33 squares (residual blocks); 29 squares used directly in previous blocks
ResNet-101.feature_extraction_layer: Global average pooling layer (output dimension N × 2048)
ResNet-101.modified_layers: Last fully connected (FC) layer removed; new FC layer added with 7 outputs connected to Softmax

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Rotate 90 degrees, left-right flip (horizontal flip), and up-down flip (vertical flip) applied to balance the HAM10000 dataset classes (increasing from 10,000 to 42,000 images, 6,000 per class).
Input resizing: Images resized to 224 × 224 × 3 for ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 models.
Feature fusion: Modified serial-based approach to combine extracted deep features from ResNet-50 and ResNet-101.
Feature selection: Skewness-controlled Support Vector Regression (SVR) used to select best features based on skewness thresholds and fitness functions.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotate 90 degrees applied to original images.
Left-right flip (LR) applied to original images.
Up-down flip (UD) applied to original images.
Operations were applied multiple times until the number of images in each of the seven classes reached 6,000, resulting in a total of 42,000 images (up from 10,000).

conclusions: The proposed automated framework using deep learning (fine-tuned ResNet-50 and ResNet-101) and machine learning (skewness-controlled SVR for feature selection) achieved a maximum classification accuracy of 95.0% on the augmented HAM10000 dataset.

Data augmentation (rotation, left-right flip, up-down flip) significantly improved classification performance compared to the original imbalanced HAM10000 dataset, with baseline accuracies of 64.36% (ResNet-50) and 49.98% (ResNet-101) on the non-augmented data.

Feature fusion using a modified serial-based approach improved accuracy to 95.0% (using the ESD classifier) but increased computational time, whereas feature selection using skewness-controlled SVR reduced computational time significantly (to 1367 seconds for ESD) at the cost of accuracy (91.7%).

The Cubic SVM (CSVM) classifier achieved the highest accuracy among individual deep feature experiments (92.7% with ResNet-50 and 92.1% with ResNet-101), while the Ensemble Subspace Discriminative (ESD) classifier performed best in the feature fusion experiment.
The proposed method demonstrated improved accuracy compared to recent studies, such as Khan et al. (88.5%) and Sevli (91.5%).

The study acknowledges that while feature fusion improves performance, it increases computational time, and feature selection reduces time but degrades accuracy; future work suggests using new datasets (ISBI 2020, ISIC 2020) and refining fusion/selection processes.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to be highly imbalanced; the authors artificially balanced the classes via aggressive data augmentation (rotation and flips) to create a synthetic dataset of 42,000 images, which may not reflect real-world clinical distribution.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the augmented HAM10000 dataset, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed method and recent studies, relying solely on accuracy metrics.

There is a discrepancy in reported accuracy: the abstract and conclusion state 91.7% for feature selection and 95.0% for fusion, while Section 3 mentions a maximum accuracy of 93.20% for an individual model (ResNet-101) and 92.83% for an ensemble, creating confusion regarding the best-performing configuration.

The data leakage risk is high because the augmentation process (rotation and flips) was applied to balance classes before the train-test split, potentially allowing information from the test set to influence the training distribution if the split was not strictly defined on original images first.
Computational time is reported for some experiments but not consistently across all models and configurations, making fair efficiency comparisons difficult.
The study compares against limited recent studies (Khan et al. and Sevli) rather than a comprehensive benchmark of state-of-the-art methods, which may overstate the relative performance improvement.
